script:
Hello, this is The Portfolio Podcast for QQQ. Tuesday — here is your QQQM recap. The portfolio closed at ninety-eight thousand eight hundred sixty dollars and thirteen cents, down one thousand one hundred thirty-nine dollars and eighty-seven cents on the session. That puts us one point one four percent below the one hundred thousand dollar base. A rough day, and the reasons are specific.

Samsung reported record quarterly profit — and the chip sector sold off anyway. That is not a misprint. Blowout earnings from one of the world's largest semiconductor manufacturers triggered a classic sell-the-news reaction across the space. Then Reuters landed a report mid-session: China's DeepSeek is developing its own AI inference chip. Two catalysts, one direction — down.

No broad macro data was available for Tuesday's session. The selloff was sector-specific, concentrated in semiconductors and chip equipment names, while communication services and select consumer names held up or advanced. The Nasdaq Composite finished the session modestly mixed, with the divergence between chip names and the rest of the index as stark as it has been in recent weeks. The damage in this portfolio was driven almost entirely by exposure to the semiconductor supply chain — from memory to equipment makers.

The dominant story of Tuesday's session was the Samsung-triggered chip selloff, and Micron Technology sat at the center of it. Samsung Electronics reported record quarterly profit — the kind of number that, under normal circumstances, would lift the entire memory complex. Instead, investors used the news as an exit. The logic: much of the good news in memory had already been priced in, and Samsung's blowout print confirmed the narrative rather than extended it. Then came the Reuters report that DeepSeek is building its own AI inference chip. That introduced a structural threat to the AI hardware demand story that has powered memory names this year. Micron closed down five point three one percent on the session, shedding fifty-one dollars and seventy-nine cents to close at nine hundred twenty-three dollars and seventy-seven cents. For context, a Seeking Alpha analysis published Tuesday noted that Appaloosa Management's David Tepper had powered significant first-half gains through a Micron position tied directly to the AI memory trade — gains now facing a stress test. Micron's weighting at five point seven five percent of the portfolio made this the single largest individual contributor to Tuesday's decline. What to watch: any follow-through from DeepSeek's chip development timeline and Micron's next guidance commentary.

Turning to Nvidia, which carries the largest single weight in the portfolio at seven point six percent. Nvidia closed at one hundred ninety-five dollars and ninety-four cents, up one dollar and eleven cents, a gain of zero point five seven percent. That relative resilience against the chip tape is worth noting. Nvidia was named in coverage connecting the Samsung earnings reaction and the DeepSeek inference chip report, yet it held positive ground. CNBC's morning segment featured Steve Grasso, Warren Pies, and Doug Boneparth all citing the same signal — Samsung's blowout followed by a selloff as a warning for chip bulls. Nvidia absorbed that warning better than most. The DeepSeek inference chip story is longer-term in nature, and the market may be distinguishing between near-term AI infrastructure demand — where Nvidia still dominates — and future competitive pressure.

Apple closed at three hundred eleven dollars and forty-eight cents, up two dollars and eighty-five cents, a gain of zero point nine two percent. News flow around Apple on Tuesday was largely tangential. A Seeking Alpha piece framed a direct comparison between Apple and Micron, arguing that while Apple posted a record one hundred eleven point one eight four billion dollar March quarter, Micron's data center memory demand makes it the stronger AI hardware play near-term. Separately, BlackRock announced the launch of a cheaper Nasdaq-one hundred ETF, a development worth monitoring for fund flow dynamics across the index. Apple's price action Tuesday was constructive — the stock moved higher against a mixed tape.

Over at Alphabet, the parent company of Google, shares closed at three hundred sixty-six dollars and seventy-nine cents, up six dollars and eighty-eight cents, a gain of one point nine one percent. Alphabet was the standout performer among the primary holdings on Tuesday. A Seeking Alpha analysis argued the stock could be fifteen percent undervalued, citing strong momentum in the Gemini AI platform and Google Cloud, backed by an eighty billion dollar equity raise and first-quarter revenue of four hundred twenty-two point five billion dollars with net income of one hundred sixty point two billion dollars. The UK's Competition and Markets Authority has imposed new conduct requirements on Google Search, but the market appeared to look past regulatory friction and toward the AI and cloud growth trajectory. Sergey Brin's reported sale of a Manhattan real estate stake at a ninety-four percent loss generated headlines but carries no direct read-through to Alphabet's operating business.

Microsoft closed at three hundred eighty-eight dollars and sixty-eight cents, down one dollar and eighty-one cents, a loss of zero point four six percent. The move was modest and largely in line with broader software-sector softness. No company-specific news drove the decline. Microsoft's four point five two percent weighting means the drag was contained, but the stock underperformed Alphabet on a day when both were navigating the same AI infrastructure narrative.

Amazon closed at two hundred forty-five dollars and thirty-eight cents, up two dollars and seventy-two cents, a gain of one point one two percent. A Wall Street Journal report published Tuesday noted Amazon is planning a twenty-five billion dollar bond sale to fund continued AI and data-center expansion. That capital deployment story is consistent with Amazon's cloud infrastructure buildout and provided support for the shares on an otherwise difficult tape.

Advanced Micro Devices closed at five hundred eleven dollars and five cents, down six dollars and seventy-seven cents, a loss of one point three one percent. The DeepSeek inference chip report weighed on the name alongside the broader chip selloff. Advanced Micro Devices has positioned its data center GPU portfolio as a credible alternative in the AI compute market, making it more exposed than most to any sign of demand-side disruption from competitive chip development.

Tesla closed at four hundred four dollars and thirty-two cents, up ten dollars and eighty-seven cents, a gain of two point seven six percent. No company-specific news drove the move. Tesla's relative strength on a down session for technology names suggests some rotation toward the consumer discretionary side of the portfolio. It was one of the cleaner gains of the day.

Intel closed at one hundred nine dollars and thirty-seven cents, down ten dollars and ninety-eight cents, a loss of nine point one two percent. That is the steepest percentage decline among the primary holdings on Tuesday. Intel carries a two point nine percent weight, and a nine-plus percent move in either direction is consequential. No single headline explains the full magnitude of the drop, but the Samsung-DeepSeek dynamic hit legacy semiconductor names disproportionately hard.

One secondary holding warrants direct attention. Meta Platforms closed at six hundred seventeen dollars and fourteen cents, up thirty-four dollars and twenty-four cents — a gain of five point eight seven percent. That is the largest single-name gain across all tracked holdings on Tuesday. Meta's two point six six percent weight in the portfolio meant this move provided a partial offset to the semiconductor losses. No single news item drove the spike, but Meta's momentum in AI-driven advertising and its platform engagement metrics have kept institutional interest elevated.

Three data points to track in the next forty-eight hours. First, any follow-up reporting on DeepSeek's AI inference chip development timeline — this story moved markets Tuesday and additional detail would directly reprice semiconductor exposure across the portfolio. Second, watch for any commentary from Applied Materials or Lam Research management, both of which fell sharply Tuesday — Applied Materials down nine point zero six percent, Lam Research down eight point two zero percent — as chip equipment names bore the heaviest selling pressure. Third, monitor Amazon's bond offering terms when they are finalized, as the twenty-five billion dollar raise will offer a real-time read on institutional appetite for large-cap tech debt at current rates.

This is The Portfolio Podcast for QQQ. We will be back same time the next business day with tomorrow's news. Watch the semiconductor tape closely — the DeepSeek inference chip story is not a one-day event, and how the market continues to price that risk will set the tone for the rest of the week.
